transcript: On October 4th, 2023, around 10:40 p.m., Ramsey County dispatch received a panicked 911 call from an IT employee, reporting two men wandering on the second floor of their office building.
It was well past working hours and no one was supposed to be in the building.
At 10:38 that night, 38-year-old Jeffrey Sanders and 34-year-old Brett Iska were seen at outside an office building on Bandana Boulevard, attempting to unlock the door.
Within moments, they had forced their way inside.
They moved cautiously through the building, dressed in jackets and jeans, their faces fully covered.
Unaware of the CCTV cameras capturing their moves, the burglars continued on.
After only a few minutes, they had grabbed numerous laptops and other electronic devices valued at more than $10,000.

When officers asked Sanders about the whereabouts of his accomplice, he denied knowing anything.
Hey, where's your buddy?
He's the only one I saw come out.
The officers then proceeded with searching his bag.
It contained laptops stolen from the building and a screwdriver inside that had been used to break in the building.
Unknown to officers who chased after him, Sanders was in possession of a Glock handgun that was previously reported stolen out of Minneapolis.
He initially refused to speak to officers or reveal his side of the story.
Sanders was previously charged with possession of a controlled substance in 2016 and was legally ineligible to possess firearms.
Both men were eventually found guilty of the burglary.
Sanders was ordered to serve a sentence of five years for possession of a firearm.
He was also given a 33-month sentence for burglary, but the sentences are being served together.
Iska was convicted and sentenced to 17 months for receiving stolen property, but as long as he complies with his probation, he won't serve any prison time.
These two burglars might have gotten away with their high-tech haul if it wasn't for the alert IT employee and the up-to-date network of security cameras.
